name: Roswell Debris Field
description: The Roswell Debris Field refers to the area near Roswell, New Mexico, where alleged wreckage from an unidentified flying object (UFO) was reportedly discovered in the summer of 1947. Proponents of the UFO theory claim that the debris was recovered from a crashed alien spacecraft and that the U.S. government has actively covered up the incident ever since. The initial reports involved a rancher, W.W. Brazel, finding unusual materials on his property. The Roswell Army Air Field (RAAF) initially issued a press release stating they had recovered a "flying disc," but quickly retracted it, claiming the debris was from a weather balloon. This retraction fueled decades of speculation and conspiracy theories. Key evidence cited by proponents includes eyewitness accounts of strange materials, alleged government suppression of information, and inconsistencies in official explanations. Some witnesses describe metallic materials with unusual properties, I-beams with strange symbols, and the recovery of alien bodies. Mainstream scholarly perspective overwhelmingly attributes the debris to Project Mogul, a top-secret government project using high-altitude balloons to detect Soviet nuclear tests. Subsequent investigations, including by the U.S. Air Force, support the Project Mogul explanation, citing balloon debris and the misidentification of crash test dummies as alien bodies. Critics point to the evolution of the story over time, with initial reports focusing on debris and later accounts adding increasingly sensational details. The Roswell incident remains a cornerstone of UFO lore and a prime example of government conspiracy theories.
subtitle: Alleged UFO crash site near Roswell, NM

claims.claim: The Roswell debris was from a crashed alien spacecraft.
claims.evidence: Eyewitness accounts of unusual materials and alleged alien bodies recovered at the site.
claims.criticism: Mainstream explanation attributes debris to Project Mogul balloon and misidentification of crash test dummies.

claims.claim: The U.S. government is covering up the true nature of the Roswell incident.
claims.evidence: Initial press release about a "flying disc" followed by a retraction, and alleged suppression of witness testimonies.
claims.criticism: Lack of concrete evidence supporting a cover-up; explanations exist for inconsistencies in official reports.

claims.claim: The debris possessed advanced technology not of this Earth.
claims.evidence: Descriptions of metallic materials with unique properties and I-beams with unknown symbols.
claims.criticism: Materials described are consistent with components of high-altitude balloons and weather instruments of the era; no verifiable alien technology has been recovered.

keyFigures.name: Jesse Marcel
keyFigures.role: RAAF intelligence officer who initially handled the debris.

keyFigures.name: Stanton Friedman
keyFigures.role: Ufologist and proponent of the alien spacecraft theory.

keyFigures.name: Philip J. Corso
keyFigures.role: Retired Army officer who claimed Roswell technology was reverse-engineered.

keyFigures.name: Charles Moore
keyFigures.role: NYU Professor involved in Project Mogul, whose research supports the balloon explanation.

mythologyConnections: Resembles tales of encounters with otherworldly beings or gods

timeline.event: W.W. Brazel discovers debris on his ranch near Roswell.
RAAF issues press release about recovering a "flying disc."
RAAF retracts statement, claiming debris was from a weather balloon.
Ufologists begin to investigate and promote the alien spacecraft theory.
U.S. Air Force releases reports attributing debris to Project Mogul and crash test dummies.

sources.author: Kal Korff
sources.work: The Roswell UFO Crash: What Really Happened
sources.title: Kal Korff
